coredata.dc:description: Female breast cancer is the leading cause of global cancer incidence and the fifth leading cause of cancer mortality worldwide in 2020. Breast cancer can be categorized into different subtypes according to the expression of biomarkers such as estrogen receptors (ER), progesterone receptors (PR) and human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2). The analysis of these biomarkers is also indispensable for systemic treatment decisions in breast cancer. In this review, we summarized recent progress for the detection of breast cancer biomarkers in blood utilizing nanomaterials. The attractive physical, chemical, optical and electronic properties of various nanomaterials facilitated the detection of these biomarkers and improved the performance of the detection. The detection of biomarkers in blood is relatively non-invasive and can provide useful biological information that is complementary to imaging examination and immunohistochemistry.

(B) Principles of HER2 immunoelectrochemical detection on the basis of AuNP-based rolling circle amplification reaction. a: Modification of AuNPs with HER2 antibody and RCA primer. b: Schematic representation of the preparation process of immunosensor. Reproduced with permission from Ref. [52]. (C) Cascade amplification of DSN-assisted target recycling and CHA reaction are shown schematically. Reproduced with permission from Ref. [78]. (D) Preparation of A) antiHER2/APTMSFe3O4 as platform bioconjugate and B) antiHER2/Hyd@AuNPs-APTMS-Fe3O4 as label bioconjugate (LB). Reproduced with permission from Ref. [79]. (E) Illustrative diagram of various steps of biosensor fabrication and miRNA detection; (1) modification of the GP/PPY composite on the SPCE surface; (2) deposition of adsorbed ss-DNA-21 probe/AuNPs on a modified electrode; (3) addition of MCH blocking solution; (4) adsorption of MB to catch DNA-21/AuNPs/GP/PPY/SPCE; (5) capture of the target miRNA-21 on the DNA-21 probe; (6) adsorption of MB into miRNA-21/capture DNA-21/AuNPs/GP/PPY/SPCE; (7) recording DPV responses of the electrodes before and after hybridization. head.ce:title: Nanomaterial based analytical methods for breast cancer biomarker detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Female breast cancer is the leading cause of global cancer incidence and the fifth leading cause of cancer mortality worldwide in 2020. Breast cancer can be categorized into different subtypes according to the expression of biomarkers such as estrogen receptors (ER), progesterone receptors (PR) and human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2). The analysis of these biomarkers is also indispensable for systemic treatment decisions in breast cancer. In this review, we summarized recent progress for the detection of breast cancer biomarkers in blood utilizing nanomaterials. The attractive physical, chemical, optical and electronic properties of various nanomaterials facilitated the detection of these biomarkers and improved the performance of the detection. The detection of biomarkers in blood is relatively non-invasive and can provide useful biological information that is complementary to imaging examination and immunohistochemistry.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is still a leading cause of death and a big obstacle to increasing life expectancy throughout the world. Worldwide, an estimated 19.3 million new cancer cases and almost 10.0 million cancer deaths occurred in 2020 [1]. The burden of cancer incidence and mortality is growing at a rapid rate ascribed to both aging and population growth, along with changes in the prevalence and distribution of major risk factors for cancer.

Female breast cancer now is the most commonly diagnosed cancer, with an estimated 2.3 million new cases in 2020 (11.7%), followed by lung (11.4%), colorectal (10.0%), prostate (7.3%), and stomach (5.6%) cancers, and is the fifth leading cause of cancer mortality [1]. Research evidence suggests there are several subtypes of breast cancer that respond to different kinds of treatment, grow and spread at different rates, and have varied long-term survival rates. In addition, risk factors may vary for each different breast cancer subtype. According to the main biological markers of breast cancer, estrogen receptors (ER), progesterone receptors (PR) and human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2), currently, breast cancer is mainly divided into the following 4 subtypes [2–5] (see Table 1 ):

However, the above molecular subtype differentiation is performed through breast biopsy with immunohistochemistry, which is invasive. In contrary, the detection of biomarkers in blood is relatively non-invasive and convenient, which can provide useful biological information in time for diagnosis, prognosis, staging, evaluating treatment response to cancer and for biomedical research. For example, breast cancer patients who are positive to HER2 have increased HER2 concentrations in blood (15–75 ng/mL) compared to normal people (2–15 ng/mL) [6]. During the past decades, different methods have been reported for the detection of cancer biomarkers, such as fluorescence, electrochemistry, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), surface plasmon resonance (SPR) and so on [7–11].

The rapid development of nanotechnology has significantly influenced the methods for detection of biomarkers. The large specific surface area, unique physical, chemical, electrical and optical properties of various nanomaterials can facilitate biomarker detection and improve the detection performance. For example, colloidal gold has been extensively utilized for biological labeling and combined with immunochromatography for detection of different target analytes [12–15]. The aggregated gold nanoparticles display distinct red color that can be observed by bare eyes. Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles have been widely used for enrich and separate of low level of target analytes from complex samples [16–20]. The large surface area and good magnetic properties of Fe3O4 nanoparticles makes the separation highly efficient. Polymeric nanoparticles loaded with dyes have been used as label in ELISA. Dyes can be either encapsulated into the polymeric nanoparticles or loaded onto the nanoparticle surface to improve the detection sensitivity [21,22].

In this review, we summarized latest research progress for analysis of breast cancer biomarkers according to different detection techniques. We limited our surveys on detection utilizing various nanomaterials. The breast cancer biomarkers included in this review are not just the above mentioned ER, PR and HER2, but also other biomarkers that may of benefit to breast cancer diagnosis, therapy and biomedical research, such as miRNA and circulating tumor cells (CTCs). Issues with current detection methods and challenges to be overcome are critically reviewed.

Electrochemical biosensors are detection devices that combine the sensitivity of electrochemical sensors with the high selectivity of biomolecular recognition. Recognition units such as antibodies [23] and nucleic acids [24] binds with the analytes and produce electrical signals related to the concentration of the target molecules being studied [25]. In general, electrochemical biosensors for breast cancer detection are mainly based on antibody, aptamer, gene and peptide sensors, as listed in Table 2 .

A key concern to be faced in the early identification of cancer is the extremely low concentration of biomarkers in biological fluids at the early state of cancer. As such, highly sensitive biosensors are needed to improve the sensitivity and accuracy for biomarker detection. Using nanomaterials as a component of biosensors has been proven by a large amount of research data to significantly improve the sensitivity of electrochemical biosensors [58–60]. Nanomaterials are often used for electrode surface modification, signal molecular labeling as well as signal amplification, and used as catalysts in reactions. Due to good biocompatibility, high surface activity and large specific surface area, modification of electrode surfaces with nanomaterials can not only significantly improve the ability of electrodes to transfer electrons, but also improve the immobilization of bioactive substances on the electrode surface and shorten the detection time [61,62]. Up to now, nanomaterials commonly applied in electrochemical biosensors include noble metal nanomaterials (Au NPs, Ag NPs) [63], semiconductor materials (quantum dots, QDs) [64], carbon nanomaterials [carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene oxide (GO)] [65] and multifarious composite nanomaterials [66,67]. Nanomaterial-based electrochemical biosensors operate in diverse detection modes, depending on the analyte under study and the type of nanomaterials. To this end, this part focuses on the application of nanomaterials in different types of breast cancer electrochemical sensors with traditional electrochemical techniques and two popular technologies in recent years, photoelectrochemistry (PEC) [31,32] and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [40,41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanomaterials for conventional electrochemical breast cancer sensors
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical sensors have been used in breast cancer detection for many years in view of their simple operation and high sensitivity. According to the type of converted electrical signal, the main electrochemical techniques used include cyclic voltammetry (CV) [68], differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) [69], square wave voltammetry (SWV) [70], chronoamperometric (CA) [71], linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) [72], chronocoulometry (CC) [73] and electronic impedance spectroscopy (EIS) [74]. Despite the variety of nanomaterials designed for electrodes, the main focus remains on metal nanoparticles (e.g. Au NPs, Ag NPs) [63] and carbon nanomaterials [64,65].

As an important part of nanomaterials, metal nanomaterials have excellent properties and performance such as macroscopic quantum tunneling effect, quantum size effects and surface effects, etc [75]. Among numerous metallic nanomaterials, Au NPs have always been valued and are irreplaceable as they not only have remarkable catalytic effects, but can also significantly speed up the electron transfer and improve the sensitivity of sensor [40]. In addition, it can be used to immobilize bioactive substances without destroying their activity. By combing antibody based recognition and DNA sequence generated electrochemical current, our group developed immunosensor for the electrochemical detection of HER2 [63]. Au NPs were used as support medium to immobilize both polycytosine DNA sequence (dC20) and anti-HER2 antibodies. In the case of target HER2 presence, an intercalated immune complex is formed between the HER2-specific peptide anchored on the gold electrode and the anti-HER2 antibody. The level of HER2 seized by the sensor correlates with the reaction between the dC20 phosphate backbone and molybdate, as the reaction forms a redox molybdphosphate precipitate generating an electrochemical current [76,77]. SWV was utilized to record the intensity of the generated current and thus achieve accurate detection of HER2 (Fig. 1 A). In regard this, our group further combined the rolling circle amplification (RCA) technique on the above method by loading antibodies and RCA primers on Au NPs as immunoelectrochemical probes [52] (Fig. 1 B). RCA was performed in the presence of circular DNA, DNA polymerase and nucleotides, resulting in a long DNA molecule sequence containing hundreds of complementary replicas of circular DNA. This results in an increase in DNA phosphate concentration and leads to an increase in the electrical signal upon reaction with Na2MoO4, thus increasing the sensitivity of the sensor. Through the catalyze hairpin assembly (CHA) reaction and duplex-specific nuclease (DSN)-assisted target recycling amplification, Zhu et al. developed an electrochemical logical immunosensor which self-assembled Au NP networks on substrates [78]. In this strategy, miRNA-141, a biomarker of breast cancer, was first extracted from cancer cells and hybridized with hairpin H1 to form a DNA/RNA heteroduplex. DSN has a strong propensity to cleave double-stranded DNA or DNA/RNA hybrids of more than 10 base pairs, whereas it has a low propensity for single-stranded DNA or RNA. Therefore, DSN selectively hydrolyzed the hybrid fraction after the formation of miRNA-141/H1 hybridization. MiRNA-141 will be fully released and the next DSN reaction cycle begins, thus enabling amplification of the target cycle. In addition, the released H1 acts as a connector triggering the CHA reaction, and large DPV responses are observed by using a two-step approach (incubation of the linker with hairpin DNA (H2) immobilized electrodes followed by the adding of HP-Au NPs). A larger Au NP network of Au NPs was successfully assembled on the electrodes through the cascade amplification strategy, which greatly improved detection sensitivity. Additionally, owing to the specific recognition of base pairing and the strong inclination of DSNs to cleave DNA/RNA hybrid strands, this method can only be triggered by specific RNA, ensuring specificity of the detected markers (Fig. 1 C). Saber et al. developed a sandwich-type electrochemical biosensor for HER-2 detection using metallic nanomaterials [79]. In this assay, anti-HER-2-Fe3O4 NPs were modified on GCE to be a platform and anti-HER-2/Hyd@Au NPs-Fe3O4 NPs were dropped onto the electrode surface as an electric label. DPV responses, derived from Ag NPs deposited on Au NPs surfaces were used for quantitative analysis of HER-2. They combined the advantages of simple and rapid separation of small volume biological conjugates from solution by magnetic materials and high binding affinity of modified magnetic materials to HER-2, providing an alternative method that is more efficient and sensitive. In fact, the use of magnetic nanocomposites improves the performance of biosensors because the interaction of detection probes with functional Fe3O4 NPs can occur in solution rather than only on the electrode surface, which increases the contact time and exposure area. It is worth mentioning that the obtained bioconjugates are separated from the solution and fixed on the electrode surface by magnetic isolation, which greatly improves the enrichment efficiency (Fig. 1 D).

Carbon nanomaterials are one of the most commonly used compounds in biosensors in accordance with their cost and superior stability. In general, electrodes prepared by carbon structures have negligible background signals, wide potential ranges and excellent reproducibility [33,34]. For instance, Ounnunkad et al. recently developed a graphene-based label free electrochemical biosensor to identify miRNA-21(miR-21) [80]. In this study, methylene blue (MB) was used as the signal molecule and graphene-polypyrrole (GP-PPY) composite was acted as signal enhancement material. DPV measurements were carried out to quantitatively analyze miR-21. PPY is a promising support because its surface morphology and charge properties can be improved by modifying different materials, and GP on the electrode surface can improve the surface area of the electrode platform and speed up electron transfer. Dropping GP-PPY composites on screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCE) improved the electron transfer performance of the electrodes. In addition, AuNPs were well distributed on PPY particles and GP/PPY composites, which could enrich more DNA probes on the sensing surface, leading to a further improvement in sensitivity with an ultra-low limit of detection of 0.020 fM for miR-21 (Fig. 1 E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanomaterials in PEC-based breast cancer sensors
ce:section.ce:para: PEC detection technology is a new analytical method based on photoelectric chemistry that is capable of detecting various biomolecules with high sensitivity. Different from conventional electrochemical methods, photoactive substances convert light signals into electrical signals after being excited by light. In this regard, interference of the background signal can be avoided to a large extent due to different excitation signals and output signals [35–37]. The improvement of photocurrent conversion efficiency is beneficial to improve the sensitivity of photoelectric chemical sensors. Consequently, the selection of photoelectric active materials is crucial for sensitive detection of biomarker.

Metal oxide semiconductor materials are the most widely used nanomaterials in PEC and have the advantages of good biocompatibility, stable optical properties and high electron mobility. The most frequently investigated metal oxides mainly include TiO2 and ZnO [33,37]. While, other transition metal oxides such as lead oxide (PbO), iron oxide (Fe2O3), cobalt oxide (Co3O4), nickel oxide (NiO) and iridium oxide (IrO2) are not widely used due to their own defects [81]. Yu et al. designed a rotatable paper photocontrollable switch to form a paper-based addressable PEC sensor for detection of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) [82]. TiO2 is a typical photoactive material with a band gap of 3.2 eV, which is nontoxic and cheap, and is considered as a promising photoactive material. To obtain high-power paper-based PEC devices, they designed a high-throughput detection mode and multi-signal amplification strategy based on TiO2. A novel cascade photoactive interface was fabricated based on TiO2 nanotubes/Pt and n-doped carbon dot/TiO2 nanotubes. Their work is divided into three main parts, (1) growing the cascaded TiO2 photoactive interface on paper fibers; (2) designing a paper-based addressable PEC chip for high-throughput detection; and (3) screening suitable target chains to trigger the hybrid chain reaction (HCR) on the electrode surface to substantially boost the detection sensitivity. In this process, a breakthrough in high-throughput PEC detection on paper chips was achieved. In addition, the cascaded photoactive interface of numerous nanomaterials with multiple signal amplification strategies is skillfully combined with the cellulose surface, which greatly improves the sensor performance (Fig. 2 A).

QDs are another kind of semiconductor material commonly used in PEC and has good photostability and long fluorescence life [32,33]. The unique property of quantum dots is their own quantum effect. When the particle size reaches the nanometer scale, they can display many physical and chemical properties that are different from those of macroscopic materials. Chen et al. prepared porous Cu2O–CuO nanoflower structured materials with uniform size as well as high surface area and further used them as signal probes to develop a DNA-based PEC biosensor for VEGF165 detection [83]. As a narrow band gap (2.2 eV) p-type metal oxide, Cu2O has attracted much attention due to its outstanding advantages such as low toxicity, low cost, high abundance and high photoelectric conversion efficiency. However, the poor stability and relatively fast compounding rate limit its application. CdS quantum dots are first immobilized onto the ITO glass surface by electrostatic binding, and the specific aptamer of VEGF165 hybridizes with DNA strand 1 (DNA s1) to form APt-DNA duplexes in solution. In the presence of the target VEGF165, the above duplex is broken to release free DNA s1, which continues to hybridize with hairpin DNA1 (HP1) immobilized on the ITO surface and triggers CHA process in the presence of biotin-labeled hairpin DNA2 (bio-HP2), producing a large amount of HP1/bio-HP2 double-stranded DNA on the ITO surface. Finally, streptavidin-labeled CuO–Cu2O nanoflowers were introduced onto the ITO electrode surface due to the interaction between biotin and streptavidin. In this study, CuO improves the stability of Cu2O on one side and reduces the complexation of photogenerated electrons and holes on the other side. Furthermore, the CdS-modified ITO electrode surface exhibited a strong anodic photocurrent due to its excellent photoelectric conversion performance. In particular, this work realized the photocurrent polarity conversion of Cu2O–CuO composites to CdS quantum dot/ITO electrodes, which greatly improved the anti-interference ability and provided a new idea for building a more sensitive PEC sensing platform (Fig. 2 B).

Current photoelectric materials still have many deficiencies in the practical application in PEC biosensors, including low photoelectric conversion efficiency, poor PEC stability, etc. Only if more photocatalytic materials with higher quantum efficiency suitable for photoelectrochemical bioanalysis are found can the sensitivity of photoelectrochemical biosensors be promoted and the potential of photoelectrochemical biosensors be enhanced to a greater extent. In regard this, several new carbon nanomaterials have been gradually discovered and applied to photoelectrochemical sensors, such as carbon nanotubes and MXenes [84]. Our group proposed a hexagonal carbon nitride tubes (HCNT)-based PEC biosensor for circulating tumor cells (CTCs) detecting [85]. CTCs are cells that shed from the primary tumor site into the bloodstream and carried in the body through the peripheral blood. These cells serve as biomarkers of cancer, indicating the presence of tumors, as well as their genetic status and drug sensitivity. In this research, magnetic Fe3O4 nanospheres (MNs) and Cu2O-aptamers were used to isolate CTCs cells and signal amplification, respectively. MCF-7 cells were specifically recognized by antibodies on the surface of magnetic Fe3O4, enabling the enrichment and isolation of CTCs cells from blood. Once the enriched MCF-7 cells were immobilized on the electrode, the photocurrent intensity of HCNT was reduced due to the spatial site resistance generated by MCF-7 cells. Then, the aptamer-Cu2O complex specifically recognized by the mucin protein 1 (MUC1) protein on the surface of the MCF-7 cell membrane was subsequently anchored on the surface of electrode. As a result of competition of light absorption, Cu2O accelerates the decrease in photocurrent, thus enabling sensitive detection (Fig. 2 C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanomaterials for ECL breast cancer sensors
ce:section.ce:para: ECL is a luminescence phenomenon caused by the electron transfer of reactants accompanying chemical reactions on the electrode surface, which has the advantages of both electrochemistry and chemiluminescence [75]. On the one hand, compared with photoluminescence, ECL effectively avoids the interference of the background light source and therefore has higher detection sensitivity. On the other hand, compared with chemiluminescence, ECL can only produce luminescence under electrochemical excitation, which is more controllable.

QDs with varied dimensions, shapes and chemical constituents are promising for a wide range of applications in ECL. Many quantum dots with excellent optoelectronic properties that are closely related to their size and material composite have great application potential [86]. Nevertheless, as ECL sensors are gradually being developed for intracellular detection, the toxicity of semiconductor QD-based materials still needs to be carefully considered. Therefore, quantum dots with shell structures have been gradually developed to improve their biocompatibility and ECL performance. Zhou et al. [87] prepared CdS:Mn quantum dots and constructed an efficient and sensitive ECL biosensing platform on the basis of dual catalytic hairpin assembly (DCHA) for the analysis of miRNA-21 and MUC1. CHA involves the following two processes. First, miRNA-21 triggers the enrichment of quantum dots on the electrode surface, leading to a significant enhancement of the ECL signal, reflecting the concentration of miRNA. Then MUC1 triggers the Au NPs approaching the quantum dots on the electrode, thus the resonance energy transfer between these two leads to a weakening of the ECL intensity, which can quantify the MUC1 level. This single sensing platform realized two breast cancer-related biomarker detection, providing a rational approach for breast cancer diagnosis (Fig. 3 A).

A variety of metal nanomaterials, such as platinum and gold nanomaterials, have been studied in the field of ECL sensors for many years in terms of their favorable electrical conductivity, large surface area and excellent chemical stability [45,46]. In addition, with the discovery of the ECL behavior of Au and Ag nanoclusters (NCs), metal ECL light-emitting devices have been rapidly developed [42,43]. The uniqueness of these nanoclusters lies in their particular surface states, where a large fraction of surface atoms have distinct valence states. Accordingly, these nanoclusters are fabricated into diverse shapes, such as cubes, rods, nanoplates, nanoshells, and etc. to improve ECL performance. Yuan et al. [41] synthesized gold silver nanoclusters (AuAg NCs) supramolecular network structure to improve the behavior of metal cluster-based ECL. In this case, Au–Ag NC supramolecular networks were prepared by host-guest complexation of Au NCs and Ag NCs using 6-aza-2-thiothymine as a stabilizer of Au NCs and l-arginine as a stabilizer of Ag NCs. The ECL response is not only much stronger than discrete single metal NCs, but also more significant than polymerized single metal NCs. The enhanced ECL luminescence of self-assembled AuAg NCs is the consequence of the synergistic effect of aggregation induced effect enhancement and silver effect in gold catalysis. AuAg NCs with a host-guest structure were used as an efficient electrochemical luminescence group, and strand displacement amplification (SDA) was used as a signal amplifier to achieve high sensitivity detection of miRNA-21 with a lower detection limit of 4.5 aM. In addition, SPR has great potential to improve the efficiency of ECL because of its angular polarization dependent effect (Fig. 3 B). Inspired by this, Ma et al. proposed a surface plasmon-coupled electrochemiluminescence (SPC-ECL) -based polarimetric resolution multiple analysis method to detect BRCA genes [88]. Boron nitride quantum dots (BN QDs), as ECL nanoemitters are coupled with the surface plasmas of Au NPs and Ag@Au NPs. When the ECL signals of QDs are coupled with the surface plasmas of different metal nanoparticles, the emission polarization angles are different. Au NPs and Ag@Au NPs not only enhance the ECL performance of BN QDs, but also affect the emission polarization mode of quantum dots due to the SPC effect. Thus, target DNA (BRCA1 and BRCA2) can be identified and detected at a single electrode interface with high polarization resolution and sensitivity (Fig. 3 C and D).

To improve the performance of electrochemical biosensors, there is still a need to explore more nanomaterials with attractive properties suitable for electrochemical bioanalysis, especially in the field of photoelectrochemistry and electrochemiluminescence. In addition, more detailed study and design of nanobio-interface, simpler methods for immobilization of biomolecules, as well as signal amplification strategy to enhance detection sensitivity and to improve detection precision should all be seriously considered.

As listed in Table 3 , optical detection techniques can be mainly divided into fluorescence [126], near infrared (NIR) [127–130], colorimetric [131,132], SPR [133] and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [134]. Fluorescence allows for both sensing and imaging of biomarkers because of its unique optical properties [135–138], while colorimetry, SPR and SERS are more focused on the use of spectroscopy to quantify biomarkers [139–141]. NIR is mostly used for imaging disease markers in organisms as well as guiding imaging in surgery due to its lack of autofluorescence and excellent tissue penetration ability [142]. For the past few years, nanomaterials have been broadly applied in the fabrication of optical biosensors ascribed to their interesting optical properties, good biocompatibility and special catalytic properties [143–145].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanomaterials in fluorescence-based breast cancer sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Compared with other biosensors, fluorescent biosensors have the advantages of high sensitivity, high throughput, simple operation, good reproducibility, fast response, nondestructive and so on. Especially with the development of nanotechnology, a large number of fluorescent nanoprobes for breast cancer biomarker sensing and imaging have been designed and synthesized, which is of great significance for breast cancer diagnosis [146].

Carbon nanomaterials have attracted great interest in biosensor construction on account of their optical properties and good biocompatibility. It has been applied to the fluorescence detection of breast cancer with good results [147]. For instance, on the basis of DNA-labeled carbon dots (CDs) and 5,7-dinitro-2-sulfo-acridone (DSA), Chen et al. constructed a ratiometric fluorescent biological probe to amplify the targeted catalytic signal for exosomal miRNA-21 detection [148]. CDs and DSA have high fluorescence resonance energy transfer efficiency. When the target exists, a single miRNA-21 strand can catalyze the disassembly of multiple CDs with DSA, resulting in a significant change in the fluorescence ratio of CDs to DSA. With this signal amplification strategy, the detection limit is as low as 3.0 fM. Due to the self-calibration ability of ratiometric fluorescence and catalytic signal amplification strategies, the probe has a high sensitivity and a certain degree of resistance to environmental interference. On this basis, researchers quantitatively analyzed miRNA-21 in several different types of breast cancer cells (Fig. 4 A). In addition to carbon dots, graphene oxide (GO) is also widely used in fluorescent sensors attributed to its excellent optical quenching ability and large specific surface area. Han et al. developed a multi-miRNA synchronous detection platform combing microfluidic biochip with GO [149]. Attractive properties of microfluidic chips, such as high throughput, low sample volume, and precise volume control, make them suitable for the construction of various sensing platforms. π-π stacking allows GO to adsorb fluorophore-labeled single-stranded DNA to quench fluorescence of fluorophore. When the target molecule hybridizes with DNA to form a DNA-miRNA complex, GO desorbs the DNA, and fluorescence will be restored. The platform has a wide linear detection range of 1 aM–10 nM and an extremely low level of detection of 0.146 aM, and has a variety of miRNA sensing capabilities (Fig. 4 B).

Noble metal nanomaterials were used to construct fluorescence imaging sensors. Luo et al. [150] synthesized peptide-protected Au NCs with the particular amino acid sequence Cys-Met-Met-Met-Met (CMMMMM). In comparison to traditional C, Y peptide (CYYYYY) -protected Au NCs, the fluorescence intensity of CMMMMM-AuNCs was enhanced to 230%, and the stability was increased by 300%. By adjusting the pH values, an Au NC detection array capable of separating and identifying 10 kinds of proteins was established. Depending on the pH value, electrostatic interactions and partial hydrophobic interactions between the target protein and the probe achieve the purpose of fingerprint identification, and multiple proteins are simultaneously identified to improve the accuracy of detection. By using the sensor, the serum from the breast cancer patients can be effectively identified (Fig. 4 C). In recent years, Ag NCs have attracted much attention in terms of their simplicity of synthesis and excellent optical properties. As a luminescent nanomaterial, Ag NCs have found a wide range of applications in biosensing and bioimaging. Song et al. developed a strategy to image different protein dimers on the cell surface based on the near-fluorescence enhancement mechanism between DNA/Ag NCs and G-rich sequences [151]. Dark Ag NCs with HER2-specific aptamers (Apt2) will be lighted up by the adjacent G-rich sequence, producing Apt2-red-Ag NCs when the HER2: HER2 homodimer is present. Similarly, in the presence of the HER2: HER3 heterodimer, other dark Ag NCs linked to a HER3-specific aptamer (Apt3) are also lighted up representing by the G-rich sequence. This strategy provides a new idea for simultaneous imaging of different HER2 dimer states and facilitates the detection and imaging of breast cancer cells (Fig. 4 D).

Metal oxide nanomaterials have also been applied in the construction of fluorescent biosensors. Among them, ZnO nanomaterials have broad application prospects on account of convenient synthesis process, excellent biocompatibility and outstanding cell capture and release performance. Guo and coworkers developed a new type of three-dimensional micro/nano biochip by growing ZnO nanowire arrays perpendicularly on the surface of a polydimethylsiloxane(PDMS) pillar substrate with a gear structure [152]. More binding sites are provided by the gear structure for antibodies and target cancer cells, and a rough surface is provided by the ZnO nanowires for CTC attachment. The substrate with micro-nanostructure can improve the capture selectivity in contrast to other CTC capture chips. In addition, ZnO is pH-sensitive. Under the condition of weak acid, ZnO nanowire-trapped CTCs can be separated quickly and efficiently from the column with minimal damage. The biochip showed high capture efficiency (91.11% ± 5.53%) and good cell viability (96%). Based on this chip, the researchers successfully realized specific fluorescent labeling of CTCs in the samples of 9 clinical breast cancer patients (Fig. 5 A).

Magnetic nanoparticles have also been used for capturing circulating tumor cells to construct corresponding fluorescent biosensors. Pang's team [153] introduced poly (styrene/acrylamide) copolymer-mernanospheres (Pst-AAm-COOH) as a carrier, loaded with different quantum dots and nano-γ-Fe2O3 to prepare red fluorescent magnetic nanospheres (RMNs) and green fluorescent nanosphere (GNs). Two kinds of multifunctional nanobiological probes were prepared by monoclonal antibody modification. Using IRMNs and IGNs for labeling two CTC biomarkers (EpCAM and HER2), CTCs can be conveniently labeled by magnetic and fluorescent double tags, thus avoiding the tedious operation procedures. Through the integration of magnetic enrichment with a size-selective SCT chip, more than 90% of CTCs, even at concentrations as low as 10 CTCs per milliliter of blood, can be individually captured on highly ordered microcolumns. The phenotype of breast tumor cells can be accurately evaluated by using the sensor, and more than 82% of the cells can be categorized into corresponding cell subsets. At the same time, the system successfully detected the heterogeneous CTCs in seven breast cancer patients and analyzed their subsets (Fig. 5 B).

Upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) have attracted much attention in the field of biosensing and bioimaging due to their stable fluorescence and interesting optical properties, such as long lifetime, sharp emission band and large inverse Stokes shift [154]. In Zhu's work [155], DNA complementary to target miRNA-21 labeled with organic dyes TAMRA and BHQ1 was assembled on UCNPs by electrostatic adsorption to construct a fluorescent sensor, which realized the quantification and imaging of miRNA-21 in MCF-7 cells. Based on luminescence spectroscopy energy-transfer (LRET), two quenchers of TAMRA and BHQ1 on UCNPs significantly improved the quenching efficiency and detection sensitivity with a detection limit of 11.2 pM. This is the first time to construct a biosensor for intracellular miRNA imaging using the fluorescence of dual-receptor for quenching UCNPs (Fig. 5 C).

In recent years, covalent organic frameworks (COFs) have been increasingly used in bioanalysis [156,157]. Due to their well-defined pore structure and high specific surface area, other functional materials can be integrated into the framework structure to further endow COFs with biological activities. Meanwhile, the long-range ordered structure of many COFs gives them a strong broad-spectrum light absorption ability, suggesting that they may be potential receptors. Therefore, COFs can be easily used to design fluorescent sensors for disease marker analysis. In addition, many COFs have intrinsic fluorescence that can be used to track the distribution of nanoprobes in real time. Tang et al. adsorbed TAMRA-labeled survivin mRNA antisense nucleotides and a Cy5-labeled transmembrane glycoprotein MUC1 aptamer onto a nanoscale fluorescent COF [158], and developed a COF-based three-color fluorescent nanoprobe for simultaneous imaging of biomarkers at different spatial locations in living cells. The COF NPs first quench the fluorescence signal of the dye on DNA. The internalization and distribution of nanoprobes in cells can be observed in real time through the autofluorescence of COF NPs. In living cells, recognition of MUC1 with the MUC1 aptamer results in restoration of the Cy5 fluorescence signal, while hybridization of survivin mRNA with its antisense DNA results in restoration of the TAMRA signal. Based on this, the researchers successfully observed MUC1 protein on the cell membrane of cancer cells and survivin mRNA in the cytoplasm of cancer cells, and at the same time, using the nanoprobe, they successfully observed the upregulation of survivin mRNA and MUC1 in normal cells infected with cancer cell exosomes (Fig. 5 D).

Polymeric nanoparticles have also been utilized to develop fluorescent biosensors. Klymchenko's team [159] amplified the fluorescent signal of a single receptor through effective FRET of ultrabright dye-loaded polymer nanoparticles, thus converting the recognition of a microRNA copy into a response of more than 400 dyes, simplifying the experimental operation and amplifying the signal in one step. So that the target microRNA of the seven cell lines can be sensitively and quantitatively detected (Fig. 5 E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanomaterials in NIR-based breast cancer sensors
ce:section.ce:para: Although the traditional fluorescence imaging technology has many advantages, the low penetration depth in biological tissues hinders the use of visible light spectrum (400–700 nm) for fluorescence imaging. However, these limitations can be overcome by using NIR light (700–1700 nm). Unlike visible light, NIR imaging has the unique advantage of producing high-resolution images at a certain depth of penetration (a few millimeters to centimeters). Therefore, NIR bioimaging has been developed rapidly in the past few years [160].

Metal QDs have been used in near-infrared imaging due to their unique luminescence ability. Chen et al. [161] synthesized CuInSe2 QDs with tunable NIR emission peaks in the range of 920–1224 nm by the thermal injection method. A thin ZnS shell layer is coated on the surface of the QDs to reduce the surface defects and protect the Cu+ ions on the surface from being influenced by oxidation, so that the NIR-II luminescence intensity and light stability of the QDs are improved with a QY value of 21.8%, which is the highest among reported Pb/Cd-free QDs so far. Meanwhile, the excitation band of these QDs extends from ultraviolet band to the near infrared band, which is conducive to the needs of different biological applications. The QDs can be specifically recognized by human breast cancer MCF-7 cells through biological coupling with an anti-EpCAM antibody. The strong near-infrared emission enables sensitive detection of MCF-7 cells. In addition, the nanoprobe can also be used for targeted bioimaging in living mouse tumors. This shows that the NIR-II luminescent CuInSe2 nanoprobe has great application potential in the field of cancer diagnosis and imaging-guided surgery (Fig. 6 A).

Lanthanide-doped nanomaterials also have excellent near-infrared emission luminescence properties, especially in the NIR-II region [162]. Zhang and coworkers developed a manganese dioxide (MnO2) -based lanthanide nanoprobe therapeutic nanoplatform for tumor imaging and precise gene therapy [163]. The NIR-II fluorescent probe Er NPs was bound to MnO2, and peptide RGD, which specifically binds to αvβ3 integrin overexpressed in TNBC, was modified on the surface of the probe to construct a nanoprobe that could be specifically activated at the tumor site. When the probe enters the cell, the MnO2 shell degrade by GSH, and the siS100A4 is accurately released for gene therapy. When MnO2 is depleted, Er NPs show enhanced NIR-II emission, and the precise localization of tumors can be observed (Fig. 6 B).

Carbon nanomaterials are also used in near-infrared imaging. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a class of luminescent nanomaterials that have attracted much attention in recent years. Given their low toxicity and good biocompatibility, GQDs have great application prospects in bioanalysis and bioimaging. To date, the preparation of highly NIR fluorescent GQDs by a simple method remains a challenge. Zhao's team [164] prepared luminescent GQDs in one step from cis-cyclobutane-1, 2-dicarboxylic acid (CBDA-2), a biomolecule derived from furfural, by simple pyrolysis at 200 °C for 12 h. Two peaks were found in the fluorescence emission spectra of the synthesized GQDs, one of which was located in the near infrared region at approximately 860 nm. The synthesized GQDs were used to image the human breast cancer cell line MCF-7 (Fig. 6 C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanomaterials in colorimetric-based breast cancer sensors
ce:section.ce:para: Colorimetric biosensors are of great interest in terms of their low cost and visual effect. For colorimetric determination, enzyme-catalyzed reactions are usually required, for example, the reaction of H2O2 and the colorimetric substrate 3, 3′, 5, 5' -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) is catalyzed by horseradish peroxidase (HRP), and the color of the solution changes from colorless to blue, which can be used as the basis of colorimetric detection. Some nanomaterials have enzyme-like activity, termed nanozyme, can be considered to construct colorimetric biosensors [165,166].

Metal nanomaterials are widely used in the construction of colorimetric biosensors due to their enzyme-like and electronic properties. Leong et al. embedded Au NCs into mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) to amplify the signal for efficient detection of HER2-positive (HER2+) breast cancer cells [167]. Au NCs have peroxidase-like properties and can effectively catalyze the reduction of hydrogen peroxide and oxidation of the peroxidase substrate TMB with a dark blue color and a strong absorption at 652 nm, which can be used for colorimetric analysis. The large pore volume and large surface area of MSNs can enrich the Au NCs and amplify the signal, so the sensitivity of the sensor can be effectively improved. Furthermore, the surface modification of HER2 antibody makes the silica microspheres highly sensitive to HER2+ breast cancer cells. The biosensor has been applied to the colorimetric detection of HER2+ breast cancer cell line SKBR3 (Fig. 6 D). Sun and coworkers [168] developed a colorimetric biosensor through the signal amplification mechanism of Bi2Se3-AuNPs for the detection of breast cancer-related BRCA1 mutations. Bi2Se3-Au NPs as a signal unit catalyzed the reduction of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) to 4-aminophenol (4-AP) for colorimetric analysis. Using Au NPs to catalyze the reduction of 4-NP, a signal amplification of 102-fold was achieved. Bi2Se3-Au NPs assemble nanomaterials can increase that magnification to 1010-fold, greatly improving the sensitivity and reaching a detection limit of 10−18M, which is far lower than other colorimetric DNA biosensor using 0D, 1D, 2D or hybrid nanomaterials. On the one hand, the two-dimensional substrate enables the Au NPs uniformly dispersed on the surface of the substrate and prevents the aggregation of the Au NPs, thus improving the stability and catalytic efficiency of Au NPs. On the other hand, compared with stable graphene oxide and other materials, Bi2Se3 as a topological insulator is easier to be oxidized and lose electrons, further improving catalytic activity (Fig. 6 E).

Carbon nanomaterials can also be used in colorimetric biosensors due to their enzymatic activity. Yu et al. [169] used a colorimetric strategy based on single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) to detect CTCs for the first time. SiO2, gelatin and biotinylated EpCAM aptamer P1 modified Fe3O4 immunomagnetic nanoparticles (IMNs) were prepared for the specific capture and nondestructive release of trace amounts of CTCs, while SWCNTs with peroxidase-like activity were utilized for signal amplification. In addition, the three DNA sequences were hybridized with P1. When the target cells are present, the conformational structure of P1 is altered due to cell-specific interactions with P1. The DNA linked to P1 on IMNs is replaced by the cells and released from the IMNs. Because the combination of DNA and SWCNTs enhances the peroxidase-like activity of SWCNTs, the efficiency of the colorimetric reaction between SWCNTs and TMB/H2O2 is improved. The method converts the quantification of captured CTCs into the detection of released DNA, and can detect down to 10 cells with a high selectivity. Accurate quantification of CTCs was realized in the lysed blood of 12 clinical tumor patients (Fig. 6 F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanomaterials in SPR-based breast cancer sensor
ce:section.ce:para: SPR techniques have gained attention in analytical assays owing to their advantages such as high sensitivity, nondestructive and label-free analysis [170]. Although SPR techniques have been studied in the detection of a broad extent of biomolecules, many attempts are still lacking for the analysis of cancer biomarker molecules. The continuous development of nanotechnology has significantly increased the sensitivity and selectivity of this technique. The high specific surface area of nanomaterials has increased the working capacity of sensors, triggering more changes in refractive index, which in turn enables signal amplification [171]. Noble metal NPs have been the primary material of choice for SPR-based detection of cancer biomarkers because of their chemical inertness and high electrical conductivity. Various SPR biosensors based on gold nanoprobes have been reported for the detection of breast cancer biomarkers, and SPR technology based on Au nanomaterials has been continuously studied to improve SPR performance [172]. A sensor based on Au nanorods to enhance the SPR performance of the upconversion material was designed to detect miRNA-21 by Zhu et al. [173] A silica shell was coated on gold nanorods, and the upconversion nanomaterial was adsorbed on the surface of the silica shell by electrostatic adsorption. The presence of the shell layer avoids energy transfer or nonradiative decay due to direct contact of the upconversion material with the gold nanomaterial. BHQ1-labeled hairpin DNA (BHQ1-H1) is attached to the surface of the UCNPs and the upconversion luminescence is partially quenched due to luminescence resonance energy transfer. In the presence of miRNA-21, H1 hybridizes with the target thereby causing BHQ1 to move away from the upconversion material and the upconversion luminescence will be restored. Moreover, another H2 on the surface of Au nanoflare (Au NRs) @SiO2 can hybridize with H1, and UCNPs are immobilized on the surface of Au NRs@SiO2 to enhance its luminescence, which in turn achieves a great fold of signal amplification for miRNA-21 detection (Fig. 7 A). There are also fiber optic-based SPR sensors that have been investigated, and applying the SPR principle to fiber optics brings many practical advantages such as miniaturization and remote control, etc. Wattiez et al. designed fiber optic-based surface plasmon resonance (OF-SPR) sensors to detect HER2 [174]. First, an anti-HER2-ssDNA aptamer attached to the gold surface biofunctionalizes the gold-clad fiber. Subsequently, HER2 antibodies bind to HER2 proteins that have been captured on the surface by the aptamer resulting in signal enhancement due to mass effect. Thus, using this sandwich assay (aptamer-HER2-HER2 antibody), the displacement of the SPR was enhanced. Sputtered gold films on optical fibers produced enhanced sensitivity to surface refractive index changes. In addition, they investigated the effect of film thickness on SPR performance and found some drawbacks, the first being that in this work it was not possible to obtain deep SPR responses. Another possible limitation is that these fibers may receive a slight gold gradient along their longitudinal axis when placed vertically in the sputtering chamber. Therefore, determining the actual thickness of the deposited film is challenging because it is not exactly the same at the tip and on both sides of the fiber, even though precise measurements such as atomic force microscopy (AFM) can handle the thickness assessment of a uniform film. This work may have positive advances for multiplexing using different fibers side-by-side, or using bioreceptors placed along the fiber tip points (Fig. 7 B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanomaterials in SERS-based breast cancer sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The Raman effect of SERS is based on the inelastic collisions that occur when molecules interact with electromagnetic fields, in which the photons that generate the electromagnetic fields can gain or lose energy from the molecules, resulting in a change in the frequency (or energy) of the scattered photons [175]. The difference between the frequency of the scattered photon and the frequency of the incident light is called the Raman shift. However, the weak effect of Raman scattering itself limits its development, as the Raman scattering light intensity is only 10−6 of the incident light intensity [176]. Surprisingly, when molecules are adsorbed on silver or gold NPs, the inelastic light scattering of the molecules is greatly enhanced (by factors up to 108 or even larger), in some cases even enabling single-molecule measurements. These metallic nanostructures are specifically excited to form surface-isolated excitations and thus generate high-density “hot spots” that greatly amplify the SERS signal [177]. The acquisition of SERS spectral information does not require destruction of the sample, which is significant for the construction of a complete image of an individual tumor. Moreover, its nondestructive nature, as well as its ability to adapt to fiber optic technology, proves its promise for practical application in in-vivo surgery or bedside monitoring. A wide range of materials have been used in the design of high-performance SERS substrates to substantially improve the SERS signal, including metal nanostructures [178], composite nanostructures [179], core-shell nanostructures [180] and some nanometal-based hybrid materials.

Metal nanoparticles are the most commonly used SERS substrate due to their controllable shapes and sizes. Silver nanomaterials have the highest LSPR enhancement factor in the partial visible and near-infrared wavelength range and are thus of interest for in vitro breast cancer diagnosis [181,182]. To address the problems of time-consuming in vitro detection of miRNAs by existing SERS and the difficulty of distinguishing Raman spectra between target miRNAs, Wang et al. combined microfluidics and digital holography to design a three-dimensional SERS holographic chip for rapid, sensitive and multiplexed identification of human breast cancer-associated miRNAs in vitro [183]. Ag NPs@Si SERS substrates were prepared by hydrofluoric acid-assisted electrodeposition, in which Ag NPs were uniformly modified on the silicon wafers. After isothermal amplification using the Exoase III enzyme, the miRNA products flowed into the microfluidics and were then separated into specific regions of the SERS substrate. The corresponding hairpin probes on the substrate hybridize with the inflowing target and then open the loop, causing dye molecules (e.g, FAM, Cy5, ROX) originally close to the SERS substrate to move away and attenuate the SERS signal, thus enabling quantitative analysis of the target molecule with a low detection limit of 1 aM. 3D SERS holograms are rapidly generated with computer assistance within minutes, where the type of miRNA target is determined (Fig. 7 C). In addition, the use of SERS to achieve in vivo detection of breast cancer has received much attention, and several SERS-based in vivo detection sensors for breast cancer have been developed. Since SERS labeling may lead to small aggregation, uneven distribution, diffusion, and small accumulation of benign tissues in vivo, resulting in inaccurate detection signals, Song et al. simultaneously loaded two Raman signal molecules on core-shell structured gold nanomaterials, one of which is the reference signal and the other is the detection signal [184]. The ratio of these two signals was used to detect the H2O2 level in vivo, which improved the accuracy of detecting small molecules in complex humans. Core-satellite nanoprobes were prepared by forming amide bonds between ammonia-modified mesoporous silica-coated gold nanorods (Au NNRs) and 4-mercaptophenylboronic acid as well as d-(+)-galactose-modified Au NPs. The electroplasmic coupling between the external Au NPs generated a strong electromagnetic field that enhanced the SERS intensity of the Au NPs-modified Raman molecules and the light absorption of the nanostructures within the NIR-II window. In the presence of H2O2, the dissociation of Au NPs through the conversion of boronate to phenol resulted in a significant reduction of the Raman signal of 4- mercaptobenzonitrile (MBN) (2228 cm−1) on Au NPs, while the Raman signal of 2-NAT (1418 cm−1) within the nanogap of Au NNRs remained unchanged (Fig. 7 D).

Semiconductor nanomaterials are another kind of nanomaterial commonly used as SERS substrates, while their SERS performance is not satisfactory. Numerous methods have been developed to improve their SERS performance [180]. Tan et al. reduced the nanoscale ZnO to the quantum scale (non-plasma semiconductor) and constructed one of the few quantum size SERS probes for label-free in vitro SERS breast cancer diagnosis [185]. Due to surface defects such as oxygen vacancies and layer dislocations, SERS spectra exhibit a unique exponential growth phenomenon with detection limits down to the nanomolar level, which is quite sensitive in nonplasmonic substrates. The quantum probe is modified on a label-free 3D nanodendritic platform that mimics the extracellular matrix, allowing self-targeting, cell attachment and proliferation. The label-free, simultaneous multiplex SERS assay was demonstrated for the in vitro diagnosis of cancer in combination with principal component analysis (PCA) and discriminant analysis (DA). Non-plasmonic, label-free ZnO-based semiconductor quantum probes modified on a nanodendritic platform showed multiwavelength SERS capability, significant SERS enhancement, and in vitro detectability at the single-cell level (Fig. 7 E).

Optical strategies provide rapid, simple and cost-effective methods for the detection of breast cancer biomarkers. In the last few years, an increasing large number of optical sensors have been combined with nanomaterials for detection of biomarkers in blood and for imaging biomarkers in cell and tissue. However, there are still shortcomings and challenges, for example, the actual situation in human body or body fluids is more complicated, and the optical signals generated by nanomaterials are prone to be interfered, thus leading to inaccurate detection results.

Nanomaterials have been a cutting-edge research area in biomedicine attributing to their excellent electrical, optical and catalytic properties. Different kinds of nanomaterials, including metal nanomaterials, carbon nanomaterials, metal oxide nanomaterials, hybrid nanomaterials and so on have been widely studied and developed. Modification of their physicochemical properties or functionalization of their surfaces can greatly enhance their performance and broaden their application. For this reason, nanomaterials have great potential for disease biomarker detection. Although nanomaterials have been applied for biomarker detection for many years, several issues still need to be faced and addressed: (1) For commercial application of nanomaterials, the synthesis methods for nanomaterials need to be more controllable, so that the physical and chemical properties of nanomaterials are constant. (2) Non-specific absorption of molecules onto nanomaterial surface and aggregation of nanomaterials in complex samples affect the detection performance. So, it is still a great challenge to better modify nanomaterial surface so as to improve the stability of nanomaterial in complex samples and minimize non-specific absorption of molecule. (3) Currently, the detections are mainly performed in clinical laboratory. Transforming of the current detection methods into point-of-care testing will of great benefit to society and help for cancer screening. (4) The discovery of more specific cancer biomarkers will significantly help the cancer early diagnosis, prognosis and evaluate treatment outcome.

Xiaoqing Li Writing – original draft; Investigation Fanghui Ma Writing – original draft; Investigation Minghui Yang Supervision; Writing–review & editing; Funding acquisition; Conceptualization Jianglin Zhang Writing – review & editing; Project administration.
